{
  "weights": {

    "RX.2": 1.0,

  },
  "active_set": [
    "R1.2",
    "R1.3",
    "R1.4",
    "R1.6",
    "RX.1",
    "RX.2",
    "RX.3",
    "RX.4",
    "RX.5"
  ],
  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "To scope “Check this,” which should we prioritize: (A) sentence-level polish/clarity, (B) argument consistency and evidence–claim alignment (including numeric claims and avoiding overstatement), (C) novelty/positioning claims versus nearest prior work, or (D) higher-level reframing/structure of the abstract?",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.2": "A generic request to “check” an abstract typically includes verifying that the motivation, method, and conclusions form a coherent, contradiction-free argument.",
    "R1.3": "Because the text reports quantitative outcomes, aligning each claim with the stated numbers and evaluations is central to the check.",
    "R1.4": "The abstract makes novelty-type assertions (e.g., first framework, transfer), so estimating delta versus nearest prior concepts is a key concern.",
    "R1.6": "Abstracts often need concise, clear sentences, so polish at the sentence/paragraph level is a common and valuable part of a check.",
    "RX.1": "We will explicitly flag limits and uncertainties arising from the brevity of an abstract and any missing methodological detail.",
    "RX.2": "We will identify potential overstatements and avoid stretching claims beyond what the provided text supports.",
    "RX.3": "We will confine the assessment to the requested checking tasks without introducing new content or external experiments.",
    "RX.4": "Given the safety-critical control context, we will avoid recommendations that could encourage unsafe interpretations or practices.",
    "RX.5": "All assessments will be based on the provided text, with any required external corroboration clearly indicated."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: To scope “Check this,” which should we prioritize: (A) sentence-level polish/clarity, (B) argument consistency and evidence–claim alignment (including numeric claims and avoiding overstatement), (C) novelty/positioning claims versus nearest prior work, or (D) higher-level reframing/structure of the abstract?

rationale.R1.2: A generic request to “check” an abstract typically includes verifying that the motivation, method, and conclusions form a coherent, contradiction-free argument.
rationale.R1.3: Because the text reports quantitative outcomes, aligning each claim with the stated numbers and evaluations is central to the check.
rationale.R1.4: The abstract makes novelty-type assertions (e.g., first framework, transfer), so estimating delta versus nearest prior concepts is a key concern.
rationale.R1.6: Abstracts often need concise, clear sentences, so polish at the sentence/paragraph level is a common and valuable part of a check.
rationale.RX.1: We will explicitly flag limits and uncertainties arising from the brevity of an abstract and any missing methodological detail.
rationale.RX.2: We will identify potential overstatements and avoid stretching claims beyond what the provided text supports.
rationale.RX.3: We will confine the assessment to the requested checking tasks without introducing new content or external experiments.
rationale.RX.4: Given the safety-critical control context, we will avoid recommendations that could encourage unsafe interpretations or practices.
rationale.RX.5: All assessments will be based on the provided text, with any required external corroboration clearly indicated.
